does: This paper introduces target-level machine unlearning, a technique that selectively removes partial targets (fine-grained attributes or concepts) from trained models rather than entire instances or classes. The authors propose an essential graph-based approach that efficiently identifies and removes the minimal set of model parameters and training data necessary to forget specific targets while preserving overall model performance. The method constructs a dependency graph to trace how targets propagate through the model, enabling surgical removal of target information without the performance degradation or incomplete erasure that occurs when naively adapting instance-level unlearning to target-grained scenarios.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on machine unlearning, which addresses the practical need for models to forget specific training data under privacy regulations like GDPR. Prior research has focused primarily on instance-level unlearning (forgetting individual samples) or class-level unlearning (forgetting entire categories), building on foundational work in data deletion and model retraining. The paper responds to limitations in these coarser-grained approaches by tackling the more nuanced problem of target-level unlearning, which requires understanding how specific attributes or concepts are encoded across model parameters. It integrates ideas from model interpretability and causal attribution to identify essential model components, extending techniques previously applied to model pruning and compression.
enables: This approach opens new directions for privacy-preserving machine learning in real-world applications where users may request removal of specific information about themselves (e.g., a person's face in a multimodal model) rather than complete data deletion. The essential graph methodology could enable more efficient compliance with granular privacy requests at scale, reducing computational costs compared to full retraining. Future work could extend target unlearning to other domains like NLP and multimodal learning, develop theoretical guarantees about complete information removal, and explore defenses against adversarial unlearning requests. The framework may also inform federated learning and continual learning scenarios where selective forgetting of outdated or sensitive knowledge is beneficial.
